title: Distance, Dot Product, and Cosine Similarity
learningObjectives: Compute Euclidean distance between two vectors and interpret it geometrically
Compute the dot product of two vectors and explain what it measures geometrically
Compute cosine similarity and interpret it as the cosine of the angle between two vectors
Explain why cosine similarity is preferred over Euclidean distance in high-dimensional representation spaces
Identify the failure mode of cosine similarity (magnitude blindness) and when it matters

whyThisLesson.text: Having a collection of representation vectors is only useful if we can ask: 'how similar are these two things?' That question is the engine of retrieval, clustering, analogical reasoning, and feature discovery. But 'similar' is not one thing — Euclidean distance and cosine similarity give genuinely different answers, and the difference is not trivial. A rookie mistake in NLP is to use Euclidean distance on embedding vectors and get worse results than expected. This lesson makes the distinction precise so that you never confuse 'close in space' with 'pointing the same direction'.

sections.bridgeFrom: We established that vectors are lists of numbers with a geometric interpretation as both points and arrows. Now that we have multiple vectors, the natural next step is to measure how similar or different they are — which requires distance and angle measures.

intuition.analogy: Imagine two people standing in a city, and you want to know how 'similar' their locations are. Euclidean distance is like measuring the straight-line distance between them through buildings — pure spatial proximity. But cosine similarity is more like asking: 'are they facing the same direction from city hall?' Two people can be standing very far apart, but if they are both due north of city hall, they are 'pointing in the same direction' and their cosine similarity is high. Two people right next to each other but facing opposite directions from city hall would have cosine similarity near -1.
intuition.analogyLimit: In a real city, 'direction from city hall' and 'distance from city hall' are clearly separable — they correspond to different physical quantities. In a neural embedding space, neither metric is obviously the right one, and the choice depends on what information is encoded in magnitude vs direction. The city analogy also breaks down because cities are 2D or 3D — in 10,000-dimensional embedding spaces, Euclidean distance behaves pathologically (the 'curse of dimensionality'), becoming nearly uniform across all pairs of points.
intuition.text: The dot product of two vectors is often the first clue that direction matters. Two vectors that point in exactly the same direction have a dot product equal to the product of their magnitudes (maximum possible for a given pair of norms). Two vectors pointing in opposite directions have a negative dot product. Perpendicular vectors have a dot product of exactly zero. By dividing the dot product by the product of magnitudes, we normalise away magnitude and get cosine similarity — a pure measure of directional agreement that ranges from -1 (opposite) to +1 (same direction), with 0 meaning perpendicular (orthogonal, completely 'unrelated' in direction).

toyExample.description: Three sentence vectors in 3D (simplified for illustration) are compared using all three similarity measures. We treat them as if they were real embedding vectors.

steps.action: Define three sentence vectors: s₁ = [1, 2, 3] (sentence about 'dogs'), s₂ = [2, 4, 6] (another dog sentence), s₃ = [1, -2, 0] (sentence about 'mathematics').
steps.result: Note immediately: s₂ = 2 × s₁. They are parallel — they point in exactly the same direction but s₂ is twice as long.
steps.insight: We expect s₁ and s₂ to be semantically similar (both about dogs). But s₂ has much larger magnitude. Different similarity measures will handle this very differently.

steps.action: Compute Euclidean distance d(s₁, s₂) = ||s₁ - s₂|| = ||[-1, -2, -3]|| = √(1+4+9) = √14 ≈ 3.74
steps.result: d(s₁, s₂) ≈ 3.74, which is quite large. Compare: d(s₁, s₃) = ||[0, 4, 3]|| = √(0+16+9) = √25 = 5.00
steps.insight: By Euclidean distance, s₁ and s₂ (both about dogs, just different magnitudes) are 3.74 apart. This is a large distance, suggesting they are NOT very similar — which is the wrong answer for sentences about the same topic.

The dot product correctly distinguishes: s₁ and s₂ are highly aligned (large positive), s₁ and s₃ are misaligned (negative). But dot product depends on magnitude: longer vectors always have larger dot products.

Cosine similarity correctly identifies that s₁ and s₂ are identical in direction (same semantic topic, just different length vectors) and that s₁ and s₃ are dissimilar. This is why cosine similarity is the standard in NLP: it correctly handles vectors that represent the same concept at different scales.

visual.question: As you rotate one vector away from another, how does cosine similarity change? Is the relationship linear?
visual.description: Two 2D vectors are displayed as arrows from the origin. A slider lets you rotate one vector through 360°. The angle between them and the cosine similarity are displayed in real time. A second panel shows a graph of cosine similarity vs angle, revealing the nonlinear (cosine) relationship. A toggle switches between 'same magnitude' and 'different magnitude' modes to illustrate that cosine similarity is independent of vector length.

technical.text: Euclidean distance is the 'ruler' measure — it asks how far apart two points are in space. It is appropriate when magnitude matters: if you represent 'how much a word is activated' by its vector magnitude, then Euclidean distance captures both directional and magnitude differences. The dot product is an algebraic operation that combines both magnitude and direction information. It has a clean geometric interpretation: u·v = ||u|| ||v|| cos(θ), where θ is the angle between u and v. This formula tells us that the dot product is high when both vectors are long AND when they point in the same direction. Cosine similarity is obtained by dividing the dot product by the product of magnitudes, yielding cos(θ) — a pure directional measure. In high-dimensional spaces (e.g., embedding dimension 4096), Euclidean distance suffers from the curse of dimensionality: all pairwise distances concentrate near the same value, making distance a poor discriminator. Cosine similarity is more robust because it is already normalised.

definitions.definition: d(u, v) = ||u - v|| = √(Σᵢ (uᵢ - vᵢ)²). The straight-line distance between two points in n-dimensional space.
u · v = Σᵢ uᵢvᵢ = ||u|| ||v|| cos(θ). A single number summarising the degree to which two vectors 'agree'. Zero means perpendicular; positive means same-side; negative means opposite-side.
cos_sim(u, v) = (u · v) / (||u|| ||v||) = cos(θ). Ranges from -1 (opposite directions) to +1 (same direction), with 0 meaning perpendicular. Independent of vector magnitude.
Two vectors are orthogonal if their dot product is zero (u · v = 0), meaning they are geometrically perpendicular. Orthogonal vectors have cosine similarity = 0.
1 - cos_sim(u, v). Transforms cosine similarity into a proper distance metric ranging from 0 (identical direction) to 2 (opposite directions).

mathematics.label: Dot product

symbols.u · v: the dot product — a single real number
symbols.uᵢ: the i-th component of vector u
symbols.vᵢ: the i-th component of vector v
symbols.θ: the angle between u and v (the smaller of the two possible angles)

mathematics.plainExplanation: The dot product has two equivalent definitions: algebraically, multiply corresponding components and sum; geometrically, it equals the product of the two lengths times the cosine of the angle between them. The geometric form reveals the deepest insight: dot product = 0 ⟺ cos(θ) = 0 ⟺ θ = 90° ⟺ the vectors are perpendicular.
mathematics.whyNeeded: The dot product is the fundamental operation that connects algebra (lists of numbers) to geometry (angles and lengths). It is used inside attention mechanisms in transformers (Q·Kᵀ), in the projection formula, in computing cosine similarity, and in defining orthogonality.
mathematics.ifRemoved: Without the dot product, we cannot compute angles between vectors, cannot define projections, cannot formulate attention, and cannot measure directional similarity. Every downstream concept in this curriculum relies on the dot product.
mathematics.encourages: Checking whether two vectors 'agree' by computing their dot product — if it is large and positive, they point in similar directions; if it is near zero, they are unrelated in direction; if negative, they conflict.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That a large dot product implies semantic similarity. Two vectors with very large norms will have a large dot product even if they point in roughly orthogonal directions.

mathematics.label: Cosine similarity

symbols.cos_sim(u,v): cosine similarity — a dimensionless number between -1 and +1
symbols.u · v: dot product of u and v
symbols.θ: the angle between u and v
symbols.[-1, 1]: the range: -1 means exactly opposite directions, 0 means perpendicular, +1 means exactly the same direction

mathematics.plainExplanation: Dividing the dot product by the product of norms normalises away magnitude. The result is exactly the cosine of the angle between the vectors — a pure measure of direction. If you scale either vector by any positive constant, cosine similarity is unchanged.
mathematics.whyNeeded: Cosine similarity is the standard similarity measure in representation learning because it is invariant to the scale (magnitude) of vectors. Whether a model represents 'king' as a vector of norm 0.1 or norm 100, the cosine similarity between 'king' and 'queen' is the same.
mathematics.ifRemoved: Without cosine similarity, we would need to normalise all vectors before computing dot products, or accept that magnitude spuriously influences our similarity judgements. In practice, not normalising leads to systematic biases in retrieval where high-frequency words (which tend to have larger embedding norms) dominate similarity rankings.
mathematics.encourages: Thinking of similarity as an angular relationship in vector space, independent of vector length.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That high cosine similarity implies semantic similarity. Two vectors can have cosine similarity near 1 if they were trained to predict similar contexts, but this may reflect statistical co-occurrence rather than genuine conceptual relatedness.

mathematics.label: Euclidean distance

symbols.d(u,v): Euclidean distance — a non-negative real number, zero only when u = v
symbols.u - v: the difference vector — a new vector pointing from v to u
symbols.||u - v||: the norm of the difference vector, which is the distance
symbols.(uᵢ - vᵢ)²: the squared difference in the i-th component

mathematics.plainExplanation: Euclidean distance measures the length of the straight line connecting the endpoints of u and v. It treats all components symmetrically and captures both directional and magnitude differences. For unit vectors (||u|| = ||v|| = 1), there is a direct relationship: d(u,v)² = 2(1 - cos_sim(u,v)), so for unit vectors, Euclidean distance and cosine similarity carry the same information.
mathematics.whyNeeded: Euclidean distance is the appropriate metric when both direction and magnitude matter — for instance, if activation strength encodes the 'confidence' of a feature detection. It is also the foundation for many clustering algorithms (k-means) and loss functions.
mathematics.ifRemoved: Without Euclidean distance, we lose the ability to detect when two vectors are very close spatially (regardless of direction), which is needed for nearest-neighbour retrieval where magnitude is meaningful.
mathematics.encourages: Using Euclidean distance when magnitude carries semantic meaning, and switching to cosine similarity when only direction matters.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That spatial proximity means semantic similarity in embedding spaces, especially in high dimensions where the curse of dimensionality makes all distances nearly equal.

methodComparison.question: Which similarity measure should you use to find the most semantically similar sentence to a query sentence?

methods.name: Euclidean distance
methods.answer: Find the sentence whose embedding is geometrically closest to the query embedding. This is sensitive to magnitude: sentences that happen to have longer embedding vectors will be penalised even if they are semantically identical. Empirically gives worse retrieval results than cosine similarity in standard NLP benchmarks.

methods.name: Dot product
methods.answer: Find the sentence with the highest dot product with the query. This rewards BOTH directional similarity AND large vector magnitude. Biases retrieval toward high-frequency items that tend to have larger embedding norms. Used in some recommendation systems where 'popularity' is a feature.

methods.name: Cosine similarity
methods.answer: Find the sentence with the smallest angle to the query. Completely invariant to vector magnitude. In evaluated NLP settings, consistently outperforms Euclidean distance for semantic similarity tasks. The standard choice for embedding similarity unless there is a specific reason to care about magnitude.

methodComparison.practicalImplication: In SetConCA, when comparing set representations to evaluate whether two sets encode similar concepts, cosine similarity is the appropriate default measure. This is consistent with SAE feature comparison methodology, where features are represented as unit direction vectors and compared by cosine similarity.

evidence.claim: In evaluated NLP retrieval tasks, cosine similarity on embedding vectors outperforms Euclidean distance as a similarity measure.
evidence.label: [Paper Finding]
evidence.paraphrase: Sentence-BERT and related work found that cosine similarity on sentence embeddings consistently outperforms Euclidean distance on standard semantic textual similarity benchmarks, in the evaluated settings on those datasets.

evidence.claim: In high-dimensional spaces, pairwise Euclidean distances between random points become nearly equal (the curse of dimensionality), making distance a poor discriminator.
evidence.label: [Mathematical Result]
evidence.paraphrase: As dimension n increases, the ratio of the maximum to minimum pairwise Euclidean distance among a set of random points approaches 1. This is a known result in high-dimensional geometry that motivates using angular (cosine-based) rather than distance-based similarity measures in high-dimensional embedding spaces.

evidence.claim: SAE features are compared using cosine similarity on the feature direction vectors extracted from the decoder weight matrix.
evidence.label: [Paper Finding]
evidence.paraphrase: In Towards Monosemanticity, features learned by the SAE are represented as direction vectors (the columns of the decoder weight matrix W_dec), and these feature directions are compared to each other and to known concept directions using cosine similarity.

failureModes.name: Magnitude blindness of cosine similarity
failureModes.mechanism: Cosine similarity is invariant to vector magnitude by definition. Two vectors in exactly the same direction have cosine similarity = 1 regardless of whether one vector has norm 0.001 and the other has norm 1000.
failureModes.symptom: A near-zero vector (representing a 'weak' or uncertain encoding) has cosine similarity 1 with a large, confident vector pointing in the same direction. The system treats them as equally similar.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Check the magnitude distribution of your embedding vectors. If there is high variance in magnitudes (e.g., some vectors have norm 100× larger than others), then cosine similarity is treating strong and weak representations identically.
failureModes.mitigation: For cases where magnitude carries meaning (e.g., confidence of encoding), use dot product or a composite metric combining cosine similarity with a magnitude term. Alternatively, normalise all vectors to unit length if magnitude is purely a training artefact.
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: In SetConCA, if some sets produce weak (low-norm) pooled representations due to cancellation of member vectors, cosine similarity will still compare them to strong representations as if they were equally confident. This could lead to false positives in similarity search.

failureModes.name: The hubness problem in high dimensions
failureModes.mechanism: In high-dimensional spaces, certain vectors become 'hubs' — they appear as one of the k nearest neighbours of many other vectors, while most vectors appear as neighbours of very few points. This is a geometric artifact of high-dimensional cosine similarity.
failureModes.symptom: Retrieval returns the same few 'hub' items for many diverse queries, even when more specific matches exist.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Compute, for each item in your corpus, how many times it appears in the top-10 nearest neighbours of all other items. A highly unequal distribution (some items appear thousands of times, most appear 0-1 times) indicates hubness.
failureModes.mitigation: Cross-encoder re-ranking, inverted softmax, or hubness-corrected similarity measures. The problem is reduced by explicitly using cosine similarity rather than dot product (since dot product makes high-norm vectors hubs).
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: If SetConCA's set representations concentrate in a small region of embedding space, some representations will become hubs and systematically appear as the most 'similar' to everything. Monitoring the hubness score of learned representations is a diagnostic worth running.

setconcaConnection.relevance: SetConCA's entire training objective involves computing similarity between set representations. Whether two sets are 'matched' or 'contrasted' during training is determined by a similarity measure — typically cosine similarity or dot product — computed on the pooled representation vectors.
setconcaConnection.whichPart: The contrastive loss function in SetConCA computes similarities between anchor, positive, and negative set representations. The choice of Euclidean vs cosine vs dot product similarity directly affects which pairs are pushed together and which apart.
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: Using cosine similarity in the contrastive loss (equivalent to normalising all representations to the unit hypersphere before computing distances) prevents the model from gaming the training objective by growing magnitude rather than improving direction. This is why many contrastive learning frameworks include L2 normalisation of representations.
setconcaConnection.whichFailureExplained: If the cosine similarity between two set representations is high but their magnitudes are very different, it may indicate that one set has a confident, clear representation while the other has a diffuse, uncertain one. The cosine similarity alone would not distinguish these cases.
setconcaConnection.proposedExperiment: Train SetConCA with both cosine similarity and Euclidean distance as the similarity measure in the contrastive loss. Compare retrieval performance and the magnitude distribution of learned representations. This would provide evidence about whether magnitude carries useful information in SetConCA's representation space.
setconcaConnection.alternativeExplanation: High cosine similarity between two set representations might reflect that both sets contain many common tokens (producing similar pooled representations by averaging) rather than that the sets are semantically similar. This confound is important to control for in evaluation.

exercises.prompt: Predict: what is the cosine similarity between the vectors u = [1, 0, 0] and v = [0, 1, 0]? What about u = [1, 0, 0] and w = [1, 0, 0]? And u = [1, 0, 0] and z = [-3, 0, 0]? Compute each before reading the reveals.
exercises.reveal: cos(u, v) = (0+0+0)/(1×1) = 0 (perpendicular — no directional agreement). cos(u, w) = 1/(1×1) = 1 (identical direction). cos(u, z) = -3/(1×3) = -1 (exactly opposite directions). The magnitude of z = 3 is irrelevant to cosine similarity — only its direction matters.

exercises.prompt: Construct a pair of 3D vectors that have: cosine similarity = 0.5 (exactly), and both have Euclidean norm exactly 1. (Hint: cos(60°) = 0.5. What angle corresponds to this cosine?)
exercises.reveal: Since cos(60°) = 0.5, we need two unit vectors at 60° from each other. One simple example: u = [1, 0, 0], v = [0.5, √3/2, 0] = [0.5, 0.866, 0]. Verify: ||u|| = 1 ✓, ||v|| = √(0.25 + 0.75) = 1 ✓, u·v = 0.5 ✓, cos = 0.5/1 = 0.5 ✓.

exercises.prompt: A retrieval system uses Euclidean distance on word embeddings. It returns 'cat' as the most similar word to 'dog', but 'feline' is not in the top 100 results. A colleague suggests that 'feline' is actually a great match for 'dog' (same animal category). What could explain the retrieval system's failure?
exercises.reveal: Likely explanation: 'feline' is a rare, formal word that appears in fewer training contexts, so its embedding vector has a smaller norm than 'cat' (a common word). Euclidean distance penalises vectors that are far from the origin, so 'feline' — despite pointing in a similar direction to 'dog' — is more distant in Euclidean space than 'cat'. Switching to cosine similarity would likely bring 'feline' much higher in the ranking, since it only cares about directional similarity, not vector length.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Construct a concrete 2D example where two vectors have: (a) Euclidean distance > 10, and (b) cosine similarity = 1. Then construct a second pair that has: (a) Euclidean distance < 0.01, and (b) cosine similarity = -1. What does this reveal about the relationship between distance and direction as similarity measures?
rubric: Correctly constructs the first pair: any two parallel vectors (same direction) with large magnitudes, e.g. [100, 0] and [200, 0], which have distance 100 but cosine similarity 1
Correctly constructs the second pair: two antiparallel vectors (opposite directions) that are very close together, e.g. [0.005, 0] and [-0.005, 0], which have distance 0.01 but cosine similarity -1
Articulates the key insight: distance measures spatial proximity of endpoints; cosine similarity measures directional alignment; these are genuinely independent properties
Connects to practice: states under what conditions each metric is appropriate (distance when magnitude matters; cosine when only direction matters)
masteryQuestions.goodVersion: Construct explicit numerical examples demonstrating that large Euclidean distance is compatible with cosine similarity of 1, and small Euclidean distance is compatible with cosine similarity of -1.

researchWorksheet.claim: Cosine similarity on sentence embeddings is a reliable measure of semantic similarity between sentences.
researchWorksheet.requiredAssumptions: That the training objective used to learn the embeddings causes semantically similar sentences to be mapped to nearby directions in the embedding space; that the evaluation benchmark for semantic similarity faithfully reflects human judgements; that the relationship holds beyond the training distribution.
researchWorksheet.evidenceProvided: High Pearson/Spearman correlation between cosine similarity scores and human-annotated similarity scores on benchmarks such as STS-B and SICK-R, reported in the evaluated setting of sentence embedding papers.
researchWorksheet.strongestAlternativeExplanation: Cosine similarity on embeddings may be high for sentences that share common words or syntactic structures (since they appear in similar training contexts), without those sentences being genuinely semantically equivalent. The metric may be measuring lexical overlap rather than deep semantic similarity.
researchWorksheet.missingControl: A comparison using randomly initialised (untrained) embeddings on the same benchmark, to establish a baseline for how much structure arises from random chance in high-dimensional cosine similarity space.
researchWorksheet.closestBaseline: TF-IDF cosine similarity (lexical matching baseline) on the same benchmark, to compare against neural embedding cosine similarity.
researchWorksheet.falsifyingResult: If cosine similarity on the embeddings gave lower Spearman correlation with human similarity judgements than a simple word-overlap baseline, the claim would be falsified.
researchWorksheet.setconcaImplication: If cosine similarity between set representations in SetConCA does not reliably track semantic similarity between sets (e.g., it tracks lexical overlap instead), then the contrastive training objective based on cosine similarity may not be teaching the model to encode genuinely semantic information.
researchWorksheet.proposedExperiment: Evaluate SetConCA set-to-set cosine similarity against human annotations of set similarity for a diverse test set, and compare to a bag-of-words baseline cosine similarity on the same pairs.

bridgeToNext.text: We can now measure similarity between individual pairs of vectors. But in practice, we work with entire COLLECTIONS of vectors — a dataset of thousands of sentence embeddings, or all the activations for every token in a large corpus. Characterising a collection requires new tools: we need to describe the 'centre' of the collection (the mean), how much the collection 'spreads out' (variance), and whether different dimensions spread out together (covariance). These statistical summaries of collections are the subject of the next lesson, and they will directly lead us to the covariance matrix — the key to understanding PCA.
